agasti [viŠĻÉdhyńĀkhyaŠĻÉ agaŠĻÉ asyati; asktic ŇõakaŠĻÉdhvńĀdiňö,  UŠĻá. 4. 179 , or agaŠĻÉ viŠĻÉdhyńĀcalaŠĻÉ styńĀyati stabhnńĀti, styai-ka; or agaŠł• kuŠĻÉbhaŠł• tatra styńĀnaŠł• saŠĻÉhataŠł• ityagastyaŠł•]
1 ‚ÄėPitcher-born‚Äô, N. of a celebrated ŠĻöŠĻ£i or sage.
2 N. of the star Canopus, of which Agastya is the regent.
3 N. of a plant (bakavŠĻõkŠĻ£a) Sesbana (or √Üschynomene) Grandiflora. [The sage Agastya is a very reputed personage in Hindu mythology. In the ŠĻögveda he and VasiŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ha are said to be the off-spring of Mitra and VaruŠĻáa, whose seed fell from them at the sight of the lovely nymph UrvaŇõńę at a sacrificial session. Part of the seed fell into a jar and part into water; from the former arose Agastya who is, therefore, called Kumbhayoni, Kumbhajanman, GhaŠĻ≠odbhava,
(pb) KalaŇõayoni &c.; from the latter VasiŠĻ£ŠĻ≠ha. From his parentage Agastya is also called MaitrńĀvaruŠĻái, AurvaŇõeya, and, as he was very small when he was born, he is also called MńĀnya. He is represented to have humbled the Vindhya mountains by making them prostrate themselves before him when they tried to rise higher and higher till they wellnigh occupied the sun's disc and obstructed his path, See Vindhya. (This fable is supposed by some to typify the progress of the ńÄryas towards the south in their conquest and civilization of India, the humbling of the mountain standing metaphorically for the removal of physical obstacles in their way). He is also known by the names of PńętńĀbdhi, Samudra-culuka &c., from another fable according to which he drank up the ocean because it had offended him and because he wished to help Indra and the gods in their wars with a class of demons called KńĀleyas who had hid themselves in the waters and oppressed the three worlds in various ways. His wife was LopńĀmudrńĀ. She was also called KauŠĻ£ńętakńę and VarapradńĀ. She bore him two sons, DŠĻõŠłćhńĀsya and DŠĻõŠłćhasyu. In the RńĀmńĀyaŠĻáa Agastya plays a distinguished part. He dwelt in a hermitage on mount Ku√Ījara to the south of the Vindhya and was chief of the hermits of the south. He kept under control the evil spirits who infested the south and a legend relates how he once ate up a RńĀkŠĻ£asa named VńĀtńĀpi, who had assumed the form of a ram, and destroyed by a flash of his eye the RńĀkŠĻ£asa's brother who attempted to avenge him. In the course of his wanderings RńĀma with his wife and brother came to the hermitage of Agastya who received him with the greatest kindness and became his friend, adviser and protector. He gave RńĀma the bow of ViŠĻ£ŠĻáu and accompanied him to AyodhyńĀ when he was restored to his kingdom after his exile of 14 years. The superhuman power which the sage possessed is also represented by another legend, according to which he turned king NahuŠĻ£a into a serpent and afterwards restored him to his proper form. In the south he is usually regarded as the first teacher of science and literature to the primitive Dravidian tribes, and his era is placed by Dr. Caldwell in the 7th or 6th century B. C. The PurńĀŠĻáas represent Agastya as the son of Pulastya (the sage from whom the RńĀkŠĻ£asas sprang) and HavirbhuvńĀ the daughter of Kardama. Several ‚Äėhymn-seers‚Äô are mentioned in his family, such as his two sons, IndrabńĀhu, Mayobhuva and Mahendra, also others who served to perpetuate the family. The sage is represented as a great philosopher, benevolent and kind-hearted, unsurpassed in the science of archery and to have taken a principal part in the colonization of the south; nirjitńĀsi mayńĀ bhadre ŇõatruhastńĀdamarŠĻ£iŠĻáńĀ . agastyena durńĀdharŠĻ£ńĀ muninńĀ dakŠĻ£iŠĻáeva dik ..  RńĀm. ; agastyńĀcaritńĀmńĀŇõńĀŠĻÉ
